# Copilot Instructions for **website-mpos**

This document is intended to help any AI coding agent get productive quickly in the
`website-mpos` repository.  The project is a small **Next.js 16** application built with
the App Router, TypeScript and Tailwind CSS.  UI components are mostly generated and
styled using [shadcn/ui](https://ui.shadcn.com).

---

## 1. High‑level architecture

* **Frontend only** – there is no API or server‑side logic beyond what Next.js gives you.
* Uses the **App directory** (`app/`) for routing.  Pages are React components located under
  `app/page.tsx` (home).  Layout and global CSS are in `app/layout.tsx` and `app/globals.css`.
* Components live in `components/`.  Shared design system pieces are under `components/ui`
  (e.g. `button.tsx`, generated by the shadcn CLI).
* Static assets (images, icons, etc.) go in `public/` and are referenced by their filename
  (e.g. `<img src="mpos.png" />`).
* A tiny `lib/utils.ts` exports a `cn` helper that merges Tailwind classes (`clsx` +
  `tailwind-merge`).

Because the app is essentially a static corporate site, most changes only involve adding
or editing React/TSX components and updating Tailwind classes.

## 2. Developer workflows

```bash
# install dependencies
npm install          # or yarn / pnpm / bun

# start development server (hot reloads)
npm run dev

# build for production
npm run build
npm run start        # serves the built output

# linting
npm run lint         # uses builtin eslint-config-next
```

Open `http://localhost:3000` to view changes.  There are no tests currently, and you can
ignore `package-lock.json` noise when working with the repository.

## 3. TypeScript & path aliases

`tsconfig.json` defines the `@/*` alias pointing to the repo root.  Imports throughout the
codebase use it, e.g.:

```ts
import { Navbar } from "@/components/Navbar";
import { cn } from "@/lib/utils";
```

Always keep the alias in mind when adding new modules.

## 4. Styling conventions

* Tailwind 4 is used everywhere; global styles are in `app/globals.css`.  The file already
  imports `shadcn/tailwind.css`.
* Utility‑first class names are composed directly in JSX.  Use the `cn()` helper when
  conditional classes or merging variants are needed (see any existing component).
* `shadcn/ui` is configured via `components.json`.  Run the CLI to add new design system
  components:

```bash
npx shadcn add <component>   # e.g. form, dialog, card
```

  The generated code will land in `components/ui` and is safe to customize.
* UI components often use `class-variance-authority` for variant props; refer to
  `components/ui/button.tsx` for the pattern.

## 5. Client vs. server components

All components in `components/` are client components unless they lack state/hooks.
When you add state, event handlers or `useEffect`, add the `"use client"` directive at the top of the file to opt‑into a client component.

## 6. Public assets & imports

* Files placed under `public/` are served statically at the site root.  Reference them with a leading slash or bare filename (e.g. `<img src="/mpos.png" />` or `<img src="mpos.png" />`).
* There are no special loaders; you can also use Next.js `Image` component when optimization is needed.

## 7. Naming and file conventions

* Components are PascalCased and exported as named constants (e.g. `export const Hero`).
* Tailwind classes are composed directly on JSX elements; avoid creating helper components solely to wrap a `div` unless logic is needed.
* Keep most UI-related code under `components/ui`.  For ad-hoc pieces in a single page, place them adjacent to the page or in the top-level `components/` folder.

## 8. Using shadcn CLI

If you need a new UI primitive (form, dialog, card, etc.) run `npx shadcn add <name>` from the repo root.
The CLI will modify `components.json` if necessary and generate TypeScript/JSX files.  After generation the new components are available under `components/ui/<name>`.  You can edit them freely; they are not regenerated on subsequent installs.

## 9. Miscellaneous

* There is no custom `next.config.js` logic at the moment, so most Next.js defaults apply.
* Environment variables can be added in `.env.local` using the standard `NEXT_PUBLIC_` prefix for client-side access.
* The project uses `next/font` to load Google fonts in `app/layout.tsx`; follow the existing pattern if you add more fonts.

---

Feel free to ask for clarification if any section seems incomplete or if you need more examples.
